coredata.dc:title: Carbon/graphene quantum dot and conjugated polymer nanostructures impart unprecedented high efficiencies in routine P3HT:PCBM photovoltaics

coredata.dc:creator: Mei, Bing
Qin, Yahong
Agbolaghi, Samira
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dot (GQD) and carbon quantum dot (CQD) nanoparticles were employed to fabricate the crab-like GQD/poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT) and semicrab-like CQD/P3HT short chain, pellet-like GQD/P3HT and CQD/P3HT long chain, GQD/P3HT nanofiber and CQD/P3HT nanofiber supramolecules. The P3HT crystals were composed of face-on ((100) and (002) prisms) and edge-on ((020) and (002) growth planes) oriented chains. The lowest d-spacing values were acquired for the QD decorated P3HT nanofibers (d(100) = 13.41–13.46 Ǻ and d(020) = 3.39–3.43 Ǻ). To investigate the function of designed supramolecules in roles of morphology modifier, donor, and acceptor, the ternary and binary photovoltaics of P3HT: (Meissner and Wohrle, 1991; Meissner and Wohrle, 1991)-Phenyl C71 butyric acid methyl ester (PC71BM):supramolecule, P3HT:supramolecule, and PC71BM:supramolecule were prepared. The GQD-based nanostructures acted better than the CQD-based ones, originated from their thinner and disk-like nature, and the highest photovoltaic characteristics were detected in P3HT:PC71BM:GQD/P3HT nanofiber systems (14.06 mA cm−2, 0.70 V, 66.11%), leading to a performance of 6.50%. Thin films modified by crab-like GQD/P3HT short chain (5.47%), CQD/P3HT nanofiber (4.56%), and semicrab-like CQD/P3HT short chain (3.46%) nanostructures also reflected the high-quality devices. The systems manipulated by the crab/semicrab-like (668 and 462 Ω.cm2) and decorated fibrillar (553 and 395 Ω.cm2) nanostructures demonstrated the faster electron transport and, thereby the lower carrier recombination. Even the well-designed supramolecules of GQD/P3HT nanofiber and crab-like GQD/P3HT short chain led to the low-quality binary photovoltaics (3.68% and 2.29%) with respect to corresponding ternary systems. When the QDs and their associated nano-hybrids were employed as the electron donors, the weakest cells were fabricated and the highest efficacy was 2.01% for PC71BM:GQD/P3HT.

TEM image (top left), SAED patterns (top right), lateral size histogram (bottom left) and thickness histogram (bottom right) of crab-like GQD/P3HT short chain supramolecules (a) and semicrab-like CQD/P3HT short chain nanostructures (b).

TEM image (top left), SAED patterns (top right), lateral size histogram (bottom left) and thickness histogram (bottom right) of pellet-like GQD/P3HT long chain nanostructures (a) and pellet-like CQD/P3HT long chain nanostructures (b).

TEM image (top left), SAED patterns (top right), length histogram (bottom left) and diameter histogram (bottom right) of P3HT nanofiber (a), GQD/P3HT nanofiber (b) and CQD/P3HT nanofiber (c) structures.
UV–Vis spectra of distinct pre-designed supramolecular nanostructures.

Schemes of crab-like GQD/P3HT short chain and semicrab-like CQD/P3HT short chain nanostructures (a), pellet-like GQD/P3HT long chain and pellet-like CQD/P3HT long chain nanostructures (b), and GQD/P3HT nanofiber and CQD/P3HT nanofiber structures (c) accompanied by the schematic presentation of face-on and edge-on orientations.
Configuration of a typical photovoltaic cell and the nanostructures separately applied in the active layers (a); energy levels of different precursors in the photovoltaics (b).

J-V curves of ternary and binary photovoltaics based on the P3HT and PC71BM and modified by the CQD, CQD/P3HT short chain, CQD/P3HT long chain, CQD/P3HT nanofiber, GQD, GQD/P3HT short chain, GQD/P3HT long chain and GQD/P3HT nanofiber precursors.

EQE curves of photovoltaic devices constructed based on the P3HT and PC71BM and modified by the CQD, CQD/P3HT short chain, CQD/P3HT long chain, CQD/P3HT nanofiber, GQD, GQD/P3HT short chain, GQD/P3HT long chain and GQD/P3HT nanofiber precursors.
Bar graphs of Jsc (a), FF (b), Voc (c) and PCE (d) features for different ternary and binary solar cells.

Impedance responses from devices with the pristine P3HT:PC71BM active layers and those modified by the CQD, CQD/P3HT short chain, CQD/P3HT long chain, CQD/P3HT nanofiber, GQD, GQD/P3HT short chain, GQD/P3HT long chain and GQD/P3HT nanofiber precursors (a); the P3HT and above mentioned modifiers (b); the PC71BM and above mentioned modifiers (c).
Charge transfer resistances of all ternary and binary fabricated photovoltaic systems.
Performance details and charge mobilities for different photovoltaic devices.

head.ce:title: Carbon/graphene quantum dot and conjugated polymer nanostructures impart unprecedented high efficiencies in routine P3HT:PCBM photovoltaics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dot (GQD) and carbon quantum dot (CQD) nanoparticles were employed to fabricate the crab-like GQD/poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT) and semicrab-like CQD/P3HT short chain, pellet-like GQD/P3HT and CQD/P3HT long chain, GQD/P3HT nanofiber and CQD/P3HT nanofiber supramolecules. The P3HT crystals were composed of face-on ((100) and (002) prisms) and edge-on ((020) and (002) growth planes) oriented chains. The lowest d-spacing values were acquired for the QD decorated P3HT nanofibers (d(100) = 13.41–13.46 Ǻ and d(020) = 3.39–3.43 Ǻ). To investigate the function of designed supramolecules in roles of morphology modifier, donor, and acceptor, the ternary and binary photovoltaics of P3HT: (Meissner and Wohrle, 1991; Meissner and Wohrle, 1991)-Phenyl C71 butyric acid methyl ester (PC71BM):supramolecule, P3HT:supramolecule, and PC71BM:supramolecule were prepared. The GQD-based nanostructures acted better than the CQD-based ones, originated from their thinner and disk-like nature, and the highest photovoltaic characteristics were detected in P3HT:PC71BM:GQD/P3HT nanofiber systems (14.06 mA cm−2, 0.70 V, 66.11%), leading to a performance of 6.50%. Thin films modified by crab-like GQD/P3HT short chain (5.47%), CQD/P3HT nanofiber (4.56%), and semicrab-like CQD/P3HT short chain (3.46%) nanostructures also reflected the high-quality devices. The systems manipulated by the crab/semicrab-like (668 and 462 Ω.cm2) and decorated fibrillar (553 and 395 Ω.cm2) nanostructures demonstrated the faster electron transport and, thereby the lower carrier recombination. Even the well-designed supramolecules of GQD/P3HT nanofiber and crab-like GQD/P3HT short chain led to the low-quality binary photovoltaics (3.68% and 2.29%) with respect to corresponding ternary systems. When the QDs and their associated nano-hybrids were employed as the electron donors, the weakest cells were fabricated and the highest efficacy was 2.01% for PC71BM:GQD/P3HT.

ce:section.ce:para: Polymer solar cells, particularly those based on the poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT) as an electron donor and the (6,6)-phenyl-C61 butyric acid methyl ester (PCBM) as an electron acceptor have received ample attention in the plastic photovoltaic industry because of large area, low-cost, flexible photovoltaic devices, etc. (Hwang et al., 2008; Vanlaeke et al., 2006; Hauch et al., 2008; Günes et al., 2007; Roncali, 2009; Meissner and Wohrle, 1991; Huynh et al., 2002; Padinger et al., 2003). Despite the fact that the donor and acceptor are naturally nanostructured due to the polymer and fullerene phase segregations, the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of the optimized devices is still not high enough (Chen et al., 2011). By large, the future applications in photovoltaics, photonics, sensing and biological labeling will be in dire need of the controlled self-assembly of nanoparticles into the designed colloidal structures to achieve the specific collective properties (Caruso, 2001; Faul and Antonietti, 2003; Lin et al., 2003; Murray et al., 2000; Shenhar et al., 2005; Tang and Kotov, 2005; Alivisatos, 1996; Boal et al., 2000). A performance of 17.4% was reached in the ternary organic photovoltaics with two well-compatible non-fullerene acceptors for minimizing the energy loss (Ma et al., 2020). In another research, a PCE over 16.7% was recorded in the ternary organic photovoltaics by employing the extra PC71BM as morphology regulator (Gao et al., 2020). A fill factor (FF) of 71.6% was also reported in the ternary organic solar cells with 300 nm thick active layers (Gao et al., 2020). A critical review was also published on the semitransparent organic solar cells (Hu et al., 2020).

The quantum dots (QDs) are also promising nanostructure materials with the size-tunable electronic and optical features and completely different from their bulk counterparts. The blends and controlled assemblies of conjugated polymers and semiconductor QDs have attracted interest for various applications (Nandwana et al., 2011; Song et al., 2016; Nozik et al., 2010; Petruska et al., 2003; Qian et al., 2014; Arumugam et al., 2007) such as light-emitting diodes (LEDs) (Colvin et al., 1994; Dabbousi et al., 1995; Tessler et al., 2002; Koktysh et al., 2004), photovoltaics (PV) (Campbell and Crone, 2006; McDonald et al., 2005; Maria et al., 2008; Long and Prezhdo, 2015), and biosensing (Jiang et al., 2009). Some organic–inorganic nanocomposites were prepared by grafting the conjugated and dielectric polymers onto QDs (Xu et al., 2007; Yang et al., 2008). Some efforts have been also devoted to the light-emitting conjugated polymer nanofibers (Di Benedetto et al., 2008), dye-doped polymer nanofibers (Camposeo et al., 2009; Yu and Li, 2013), QD-decorated polymer nanofibers (Yu et al., 2011) and QD-doped polymer nanofibers (Meng et al., 2011; Zhang et al., 2012).

Among the QDs, the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) assume numerous novel chemical/physical properties due to the quantum confinement and edge effects (Girit et al., 2009; Ponomarenko et al., 2008). The GQDs with the ultrasmall size and chemical inertia have discrete band-gaps and render the typical semiconducting characteristics that are applicable in the photovoltaic and biological devices (Yan et al., 2010; Ritter and Lyding, 2009; Wu et al., 2007; Yang et al., 2010; Liu et al., 2008; Zhu et al., 2011; Robinson et al., 2011). On the other side, the carbon quantum dots (CQDs), quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with sizes below 10 nm, have received extensive studies in recent years due to their low toxicity, good physicochemical stability, facile functionalization and fluorescence properties (Wang et al., 2018; Baker and Baker, 2010; Lim et al., 2015). The CQDs are widely applicable in different types of solar cells (Mirtchev et al., 2012; Zhang et al., 2013; Liu et al., 2014; Dao et al., 2016; Sun et al., 2014; Briscoe et al., 2015; Xie et al., 2014), fluorescent biosensing (Ding et al., 2014; Chen et al., 2017), chemical sensing (Yang et al., 2014), optical imaging in vivo (Yang et al., 2009), electro/photocatalysts (Tang et al., 2014; Li et al., 2010; Perathoner et al., 2017; Lu et al., 2016), etc.

Li et al. (Li et al., 2011) used the GQDs prepared by the electrochemical approach in the aqueous phase system to completely replace the PCBM and reach an efficacy of 1.28%. Xu et al. (Xu et al., 2012) demonstrated the control of hole transfer rate in the QD/conjugated polymer hybrids by using a series of core/shell QDs with varying shell thicknesses. Gupta et al. (Gupta et al., 2011) prepared the GQDs functionalized with aniline (ANI), and the as-fabricated hybrid solar cells based on P3HT:ANI-GQDs presented a PCE of 1.14%. The P3HT nanowires and the bound QDs onto them were also utilized to reach the efficiencies beyond 4% (Xu et al., 2019; Yu et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2014; Dang et al., 2016; Ren et al., 2011). Kim et al. (Kim et al., 2013) compared the effects of GQDs with different oxidation degrees on the photovoltaic characteristics and a maximum efficiency of 7.6% was achieved. Tsai et al. (Tsai et al., 2016) combined the GQDs with n-type silicon heterojunction solar cells to expand the spectral range absorption and decrease the number of wasted photons in the UV region.

In the current research, the GQD and CQD nanoparticles were employed to fabricate the crab-like GQD/P3HT and semicrab-like CQD/P3HT short chain, pellet-like GQD/P3HT and CQD/P3HT long chain, GQD/P3HT nanofiber and CQD/P3HT nanofiber supramolecules. The designed nano-hybrids were analyzed and utilized in the active layers of P3HT:PC71BM photovoltaic cells. The GQD-based nanostructures acted better than the CQD-based ones and the highest photovoltaic characteristics were obtained in the P3HT:PC71BM:GQD/P3HT nanofiber systems, i.e., short circuit current density (Jsc) = 14.06 mA cm−2, open circuit voltage (Voc) = 0.70 V, FF = 66.11% and finally PCE = 6.50%. The active layers modified by the crab-like GQD/P3HT short chain (PCE = 5.47%) and CQD/P3HT nanofiber (PCE = 4.56%) nanostructures also demonstrated the high-quality data.

The citric acid (2 g) and ethylenediamine (650 µL) were dissolved in deionized water. Then the solution was transferred to a teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 250 °C for 12 h and, subsequently, it was cooled to 30 °C. The reaction solution was filtered and a dark filter solution was acquired, which was further dialyzed for 6 days and the CQDs were reached by vacuum freeze-drying (Zhang et al., 2015; Zhu et al., 2013).

According to the modified Hummers method (Fan et al., 2015; Hummers and Offeman, 1958), 1.0 g of graphite and 60 mL H2SO4 (98%) was stirred in an ice bath, and 5.8 g KMnO4 was slowly added with stirring for 45 min. The solution was heated to 30 °C for 2 h, 40 mL of deionizer water was added, the reaction was heated to 90 °C for 30 min, then 80 mL deionizer water was poured. When the system was cooled down to 60 °C, 10 mL H2O2 (30%) was added to give an orange yellow solution. A 200 mL of 5% HCl solution was added, the supernatant was decanted and centrifuged with deionizer water, and the mixture solution was further dialyzed for 2 days and the graphene oxide (GO) powder was obtained by vacuum freeze-drying. The reaction mixture was stirred at 30 °C for 1 h and then heated to 70 °C for 1 h. When the KMnO4 was consumed by pouring over ice, the solution was filtered with the polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) membrane and dialyzed to reach the GQDs (Fan et al., 2015).

The regioregular P3HTs (˃ 99%, number average molecular weights (Mn) = 50 000, 30 000, and 6 000 g mol−1, polydispersity index (PDI) = 1.20–1.26) were synthesized using Grignard metathesis polymerization (Lohwasser and Thelakkat, 2011). The details of preparation and characterization of a typical P3HT are reported in Supplementary Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum dot/polymer nanostructures and respective devices
ce:section.ce:para: The P3HT conjugated polymers were separately dissolved in 1,2-dichlorobenzene (1,2-DCB) at 100 °C for 45 min and then added to the pre-dispersed QDs in the same solvent with a total concentration of 0.1 wt%. The resulted vials were introduced to the sonication (30 °C, 8 h) and crystallization (25 °C, 24 h) steps. The active layer solutions were spin-coated inside a nitrogen glove box onto the poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrenesulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS) modified indium tin oxide (ITO, sheet resistance of 10–15 Ω/square) on the glass to yield 400 nm films. The thickness of active layers was verified by a surface profilometer (Tencor, Alpha-500). To complete the device fabrication, the LiF interfacial layer (1 nm) and Al electrode (100 nm) were thermally evaporated onto the device under the vacuum below 2 × 10-4 Pa. The current density–voltage (J-V) characterizations were performed using a computer-controlled measurement unit from Newport under the illumination of AM1.5G, 100 mW/cm2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum dot/nanofiber nanostructures and associated devices
ce:section.ce:para: The CQD/P3HT and GQD/P3HT nanofibers were grown from 1,2-DCB using the isothermal method. The solutions were prepared with a concentration of 0.01 wt% and a ratio of 1:10 for both CQD:P3HT and GQD:P3HT systems. The solutions were heated at 80 °C within 20 min and then switched to the crystallization temperature of 5 °C in a circulator bath and kept for 2 days. The QD/P3HT nanofiber solutions were ultimately spun onto a modified ITO substrate to reach a 400 nm thick film and the LiF and Al layers were thermally evaporated to complete the device configuration.

In this work, the graphenic and carbonic QDs were utilized to grow the supramolecular nanostructures. According to the transmission electron microscopic (TEM, Philips CM30) images, the GQDs were monodisperse with a diameter range of 2–4 nm, as reported in Fig. 1 (a). TEM image, selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern, lateral size/thickness histograms of GQDs are depicted in Fig. 1(a). On the basis of the atomic force microscopy data (AFM, Nanoscope V), the thicknesses of GQDs were distributed in the range of 0.7–4.2 nm with a height peak detected at 2.8 nm. By respecting the theoretical thickness of a graphene layer (~0.34 nm), the AFM analysis verified an average layer number of 8 for the GQDs. On the other hand, the CQDs were less monodisperse in both lateral size and thickness. The lateral size and thickness of synthesized CQDs ranged in 2–5 nm and peaked at 4 nm. The distribution of CQDs was mainly wider than that of the GQDs. In addition, the disk-like configuration of GQDs further facilitated the formation of more mature nanostructures. The respective SAED patterns of GQDs and CQDs are also illustrated in the inset panels of Fig. 1(a) and (b), respectively, which are fingerprints of partially crystalline structures.

The crystallization of P3HT short chains in the presence of GQD and CQD nanoparticles reflected the crab-like and semicrab-like supramolecules, respectively. In fact, the QDs in the 1,2-DCB at 25 °C acted as the seeding agents for the crystallization of P3HT short chains and after one day, the crab-like and semicrab-like nanostructures appeared. Fig. 2 (a) and (b) demonstrate the TEM images, SAED patterns, and the lateral size/thickness histograms of crab-like GQD/P3HT and semicrab-like CQD/P3HT short chain nanostructures, respectively. The feet of crabs were composed of the face-on and edge-on oriented P3HT fibrillar crystals. The SAED patterns with (100) and (002) prisms in the directions of hexyl side chains and main backbone longitude, respectively, manifested a face-on orientation for the P3HT nanofibrils, fabricated by the P3HT short chains. Likewise, the appearance of (020) and (002) growth planes in the π-π stacking and backbone longitude directions approved an edge-on orientation for the P3HT nanofibrils.

The disk-like GQDs as the seeding agents acted better than the spherical CQDs in the crystallization of P3HT short chains. The development of mature crab-like supramolecules with the coarser and longer P3HT feet approved this claim. Both lateral size and thickness of P3HT fibrils were more monodisperse in the crab-like GQD/P3HT nanostructures (Fig. 2(a)) compared to those of semicrab-like CQD/P3HT ones (Fig. 2(b)). In contrast, the mentioned nanostructures were common in the face-on and edge-on orientations. The overall lateral sizes of crab-like and semicrab-like supramolecules ranged in 10–25 nm and 5–25 nm with the peaks of 15 and 10 nm, respectively. Furthermore, the thicknesses of both crab-like and semicrab-like supramolecules ranged in 2–6 nm and peaked at 4 nm. However, the portion of larger thicknesses was higher in the crab-like GQD/P3HT nanostructure, as depicted in the bar graphs of thickness distributions in Fig. 2(a) and (b).

By increasing the length of P3HT chains, the pellet-like configurations were developed instead of the crab/semicrab-like nanostructures. Actually, the longer P3HT chains did not have opportunity to be further folded and be inserted into the nanofibrillar structures. Instead, these long P3HT chains were arranged onto the surface of GQDs and CQDs to completely cover them. The typical pellet-like GQD/P3HT and CQD/P3HT supramolecules are illustrated in Fig. 3 (a) and (b), accompanied by the related SAED patterns and lateral size/thickness histograms. The crystallization of neither short nor long P3HT chains affected the innate crystalline structure of GQD and CQD nanoparticles; because the respective prisms in the crab/semicrab-like (Fig. 2(a) and (b)) and the pellet-like (Fig. 3(a) and (b)) nano-hybrids were retained exactly similar to those detected in the pristine GQD and CQD patterns (Fig. 1(a) and (b)).

On the basis of SAED patterns of Fig. 3(a) and (b) for the GQD/P3HT and CQD/P3HT pellet-like nanostructures, respectively, the face-on and edge-on orientations were detected. The growth prisms of (100) and (002) in the face-on and (020) and (002) in the edge-on orientations were weaker in the pellet-like configurations in comparison with those detected for the crab/semicrab-like supramolecules (Fig. 2 versus Fig. 3). In fact, the degree of crystallization was higher in the fibrillar P3HTs, which belonged to the crab/semicrab-like supramolecules. The histograms of lateral size and thickness distributions were moved towards the larger values in the pellet-like nanostructures (Fig. 3) with respect to the pristine QDs (Fig. 1). The coarser structures of pellet-like supramolecules than those of corresponding QDs approved that the P3HT long chains have completely covered their seeds during the crystallization. The lateral sizes of both GQD/P3HT and CQD/P3HT pellet-like nanostructures ranged in 2–6 nm and also peaked at 4 nm. Furthermore, the thicknesses of GQD- and CQD-based nanostructures were distributed in the range of 3–6 nm with the maximum values of 5 nm, as represented in the histograms of Fig. 3(a) and (b).

The QD/P3HT nanofiber supramolecules were the single crystalline P3HT nanofibers decorated by the GQD/CQD precursors. The typical pristine P3HT nanofibers before decoration are illustrated in TEM image of Fig. 4 (a). The SAED patterns recorded from different parts of a typical P3HT nanofiber (Fig. 4(a, top right)) approved the four sharp growth planes in both face-on ((100) and (002)) and edge-on ((020) and (002)) orientations. The layer spacings between the crystallographic planes (d(100) and d(020)) were calculated based on Bragg peak positions (Kim and Frisbie, 2008). The d(100) values usually range in 13–19 Ǻ and, on the other hand, the d(020) and d(002) values range in 3.5–5.0 Ǻ (Zenoozi et al., 2017). In addition, distinct growth prisms of P3HT crystals were approved by the grazing incidence wide-angle X-ray scattering (GIWAXS) in our previous works (Agbolaghi et al., 2016; Zenoozi et al., 2017). The lowest d-spacing values were acquired for the P3HT nanofibers, i.e., d(100) = 13.41–13.46 Ǻ and d(020) = 3.39–3.43 Ǻ. Furthermore, the length and diameter distributions of the grown P3HT nanofibers are reported in Fig. 4(a, bottom). The length and diameter histograms ranged in 20–50 nm and 2–5 nm and peaked at 30 and 2 nm, respectively. The decorated P3HT nanofibers with the GQD and CQD nanoparticles accompanied by the corresponding SAED patterns and the length/diameter histograms are also displayed in Fig. 4(a) and (b), respectively. TEM image of Fig. 4(b) demonstrates a typical GQD/P3HT nanofiber, in which the GQDs have been decorated like the kebab pieces onto the P3HT nanofiber. The SAED patterns possessed the spots of GQD and crystalline P3HT nanofiber with together. The length distribution of GQD/P3HT nanofibrillar supramolecules resembled that reported for the pristine P3HT nanofibers. In fact, the GQDs were arranged onto the surface of P3HT nanofibers. Actually, the principle reason for the arrangement of QDs onto the P3HT nanofibers is the π-stacking and the tendency of distinct conductive precursors to each other. According to the diameter histogram of GQD/P3HT nanofibrillar supramolecules, the distribution was altered from 2 to 5 nm (peak = 2 nm) to 2–6 nm, but with a majority of 4 nm in the thickness distribution (Fig. 4(b, bottom right)).

The accumulation of GQDs considerably increased the overall diameter and, consequently, the diameter distribution was moved towards the larger diameters. Similar results were acquired in the CQD/P3HT nanofibrillar supramolecules, as depicted in different parts of Fig. 4(c). The growth planes of (100) and (002) for the face-on and (020) and (002) for the edge-on orientations were retained in the presence of CQDs. For both GQD/P3HT and CQD/P3HT fibrillar nano-hybrids, the d(100) and d(020) values ranged in 13.43–13.47 Ǻ and 3.41–3.46 Ǻ, respectively. Notably, the QD nanoparticles were not capable of destroying the structure of grown P3HT nanofibers. Similarly, the length and diameter distributions of CQD/P3HT nanofibers ranged in 20–40 nm and 2–6 nm and also peaked at 30 and 4 nm, respectively.

The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra of pre-designed supramolecules were recorded by a Lambda 750 UV–Vis spectrometer using the final solutions. Fig. 5 manifests UV–Vis spectra of the pre-designed supramolecules subsuming the CQD/P3HT short/long chain, CQD/P3HT nanofiber, GQD/P3HT short/long chain and GQD/P3HT nanofiber. As above-mentioned, the GQD-based nanostructures were better than the associated CQD-based ones, also approved through more intensified and red-shifted peaks of UV–Vis spectra. The schemes of crab-like/semicrab-like, pellet-like, and fibrillar supramolecules are depicted in distinct parts of Fig. 6 . The edge-on and face-on orientations were designated for all types of supramolecules including the GQD and CQD nanoparticles. The fabricated nano-hybrids were used to promote the function of P3HT:PC71BM active layers. Fig. 7 (a) illustrates the configuration of a typical solar cell fabricated in this work, which was composed of the ITO (anode), PEDOT:PSS (anodic buffer layer), P3HT:PC71BM pristine and modified active layers, LiF (cathodic buffer layer) and Al (cathode). The schemes of modifying supramolecules and the chemical structures of P3HT and PC71BM are also displayed in the left panel of Fig. 7(a). Furthermore, the energy levels of different precursors applied in the photovoltaics are shown in Fig. 7(b).

Herein, to investigate the function of designed supramolecules in the roles of morphology modifier, donor, and acceptor constituents, different types of active layers have been prepared. The J-V curves of ternary (P3HT:PC71BM:supramolecule) are reported in Fig. 8 (a). Likewise, the J-V curves of the binary systems (P3HT:supramolecule and PC71BM:supramolecule) are represented in Fig. 8(b) and (c). The corresponding external quantum efficiency (EQE) plots are also displayed in Fig. 9 (a), (b), and (c). The data details are tabulated in Table 1 . The pristine P3HT:PC71BM photovoltaic cells possessed the Jsc, FF, Voc, hole mobility (µh), electron mobility (µe) and PCE values of 8.89 mA cm−2, 48.44%, 0.56 V, 5.9 × 10–9 cm2 V−1 s−1, 8.1 × 10–8 cm2 V−1 s−1 and 2.41%, respectively. The addition of primary CQD and GQD nanoparticles did not considerably alter the photovoltaic features, i.e., 9.06 mA cm−2, 49.92%, 0.56 V, 6.3 × 10–9 cm2 V−1 s−1, 8.5 × 10–8 cm2 V−1 s−1 and 2.53% for the P3HT:PC71BM:CQD systems and 9.73 mA cm−2, 50.98%, 0.56 V, 8.7 × 10–9 cm2 V−1 s−1, 9.6 × 10–8 cm2 V−1 s−1 and 2.78% for the P3HT:PC71BM:GQD systems.

In the next step, through designing the crab-like GQD/P3HT short chain and semicrab-like CQD/P3HT short chain nano-hybrids (Fig. 2(a) and (b)), the differences in the photovoltaic characteristics were highlighted between the GQD- and CQD-based systems. Actually, the mature crab-like GQD/P3HT short chain nanostructures with more crowded P3HT feet acted better than the immature semicrab-like CQD/P3HT short chain supramolecules as the morphology modifiers. The data couples of (13.86 and 11.78 mA cm−2), (60.74 and 51.48%), (0.65 and 0.57 V), (3.1 × 10–3 and 2.1 × 10–7 cm2 V−1 s−1), (4.5 × 10–2 and 3.6 × 10–6 cm2 V−1 s−1) were acquired for the P3HT:PC71BM:GQD/P3HT short chain and P3HT:PC71BM:CQD/P3HT short chain devices, respectively. These results reflected the efficiencies of 5.47% and 3.46% for the mentioned GQD- and CQD-based systems, respectively. The perfect crab-like supramolecules were one of the best nano-hybrid candidates to promote the charge mobility, current density and, eventually, the cell performance.

By increasing the molecular weight of P3HT chains from 6 000 g mol−1 to 30 000 g mol−1, the pellet-like configurations were fabricated in both GQD- and CQD-based supramolecules (Fig. 3(a) and (b)). The pellet-like GQD/P3HT and CQD/P3HT long chain nanostructures were not as effective as the crab-like GQD/P3HT short chain and semicrab-like CQD/P3HT short chain supramolecules. Therefore, the quality of fabricated photovoltaic cells plummeted. The data couples of (12.14 and 10.32 mA cm−2), (57.23 and 50.12%), (0.61 and 0.56 V), (1.7 × 10–5 and 2.5 × 10–8 cm2 V−1 s−1), (2.3 × 10–4 and 4.7 × 10–7 cm2 V−1 s−1) and (4.24 and 2.90%) were reached for the Jsc, FF, Voc, µh, µe and PCE parameters in the P3HT:PC71BM:GQD/P3HT long chain and P3HT:PC71BM:CQD/P3HT long chain systems, respectively. The better performance of GQD-based nanostructures such as crab-like and pellet-like with respect to the CQD-based ones like semicrab-like and pellet-like could be originated from the intrinsic nature of GQDs, which have the thinner and disk-like configurations.

To further improve the photovoltaic parameters and, consequently, the cell efficacy, the decorated P3HT nanofibers with the QD nanoparticles were prepared (Fig. 4(b) and (c)). The largest PCE (=6.50%) was recorded for the P3HT:PC71BM:GQD/P3HT nanofiber solar cells, which was reached from the Jsc, FF, Voc, µh and µe values of 14.05 mA cm−2, 66.11%, 0.70 V, 8.6 × 10–3 cm2 V−1 s−1, 9.7 × 10–2 cm2 V−1 s−1, respectively. The higher crystallinity of P3HT inside the pre-grown nanofibers can elevate the hole transport in P3HT domain, reflecting an improved charge transport balance between the electrons and holes thereby increasing the Jsc and FF (Moulé and Meerholz, 2008; Peet et al., 2009). Furthermore, the enhanced Voc could be originated from the increased effective band gap compared to relative difference between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) (Li et al., 2007). On the other hand, the highest photovoltaic characteristics among the CQD-based solar cells were detected in the ternary P3HT:PC71BM:CQD/P3HT nanofiber systems, i.e., Jsc = 12.89 mA cm−2, FF = 58.01%, Voc = 0.61 V, µh = 8.0 × 10–6 cm2 V−1 s−1, µe = 9.3 × 10–5 cm2 V−1 s−1 and PCE = 4.56%. As mentioned previously, the better performances of GQD-based nanostructures compared with the CQD-based ones could be assigned to the disk-like and thin configuration of GQDs, which were capable of arranging more mature supramolecules by means of the P3HT conjugated chains and also being well-arranged onto the P3HT nanofibers. The bar graphs of Jsc, FF, Voc and PCE features for the discussed ternary systems are also represented in Fig. 10 (a), (b), (c) and (d), respectively.

In addition to the ternary photoactive layers, in which the supramolecules were utilized as the modifiers, the binary systems were fabricated on the basis of P3HT:supramolecule and PC71BM:supramolecule. In the former systems, the pre-designed nanostructures were in the role of acceptors and in the latter ones, they played part as the donor precursors. In both only donor and only acceptor roles, the pre-developed nano-hybrids were not as vigorous as their modifying states. Hence, some lower efficacies were obtained and tabulated in Table 1. The respective J-V and EQE curves are also depicted in Fig. 8(b) and (c) as well as Fig. 9(b) and (c), respectively. The P3HT:CQD and P3HT:GQD photoactive layers reflected the data of (6.31 and 7.17 mA cm−2), (36.01 and 37.71%), (0.48 and 0.48 V), (2.0 × 10–10 and 4.6 × 10–10 cm2 V−1 s−1), (1.8 × 10–9 and 5.8 × 10–9 cm2 V−1 s−1) and, eventually, (1.09 and 1.30%). The elimination of PC71BM molecules from the active layers and the assignment of the QD derivatives instead of them significantly decreased the cell performances. Even the well-designed supramolecules including the GQD/P3HT nanofiber, crab-like GQD/P3HT short chain, and CQD/P3HT nanofiber led to the low-quality photovoltaic devices with respect to the corresponding ternary systems. The photovoltaic data of devices in question were (12.23 mA cm−2, 51.89%, 0.58 V, 3.3 × 10–6 cm2 V−1 s−1, 4.5 × 10–5 cm2 V−1 s−1 and 3.68%), (9.54 mA cm−2, 45.20%, 0.53 V, 3.2 × 10–7 cm2 V−1 s−1, 4.0 × 10–6 cm2 V−1 s−1 and 2.29%), and (9.14 mA cm−2, 44.51%, 0.51 V, 1.7 × 10–8 cm2 V−1 s−1, 1.40 × 10–7 cm2 V−1 s−1 and 2.07%), respectively.

In another empirical design, the QDs and their associated nano-hybrids were employed as the electron donor constituents. These types of binary photovoltaic cells were very weak and the highest PCE was equal to 2.01%, which was acquired for the PC71BM:GQD/P3HT nanofiber systems (9.12 mA cm−2, 43.17%, 0.51 V, 7.7 × 10–9 cm2 V−1 s−1, 6.4 × 10–8 cm2 V−1 s−1). Through comparing the PC71BM:CQD (1.03 mA cm−2, 8.21%, 0.21 V and 0.02%) with the PC71BM:semicrab-like CQD/P3HT short chain (2.65 mA cm−2, 17.71%, 0.34 V and 0.16%) and the PC71BM:CQD/P3HT nanofiber (7.96 mA cm−2, 38.54%, 0.49 V and 1.50%), it could be elicited that the presence of P3HT chains in the forms of crab's feet and perfect single crystalline nanofibers played a prominent role as a donor. Similar results were detected in the binary PC71BM:GQD devices (1.09 mA cm−2, 9.38%, 0.26 V and 0.03%) compared with the PC71BM:crab-like GQD/P3HT short chain (3.01 mA cm−2, 20.12%, 0.37 V and 0.22%) and PC71BM:GQD/P3HT nanofiber (9.12 mA cm−2, 43.17%, 0.51 V and 2.01%) systems. The bar graphs of Jsc, FF, Voc and PCE features for all ternary and binary photoactive layers are drawn in Fig. 10(a), (b), (c) and (d), respectively.

The EQE spectra were measured using the thin film photoactive layers by a system equipped with a Xe lamp (200 W), a monochromator (CVI), a calibrated photodetector (Newport 818), a lens module, and an electrometer (Keithley 237). The peak values of 94–96% at 634 nm and 86–89% at 631 nm were detected for the P3HT:PC71BM:GQD/P3HT nanofiber and P3HT:PC71BM:GQD/P3HT short chain, respectively. These EQE data were higher than those detected for the other systems (Fig. 9). The CQD/P3HT nanofiber (peak values of 83–86% at 630 nm) and pellet-like GQD/P3HT long chain (peak values of 82–84% at 625 nm) supramolecules also improved the EQE results but not as much as the GQD/P3HT nanofiber and crab-like GQD/P3HT short chain nano-hybrids, as shown in the EQE curves of Fig. 9. The peak values and intensities of EQE curves recorded for the binary systems were considerably less than those detected in the respective ternary photoactive layers (Fig. 9(b) and (c) versus Fig. 9(a)). The best results were collected for the P3HT:GQD/P3HT nanofiber devices (78–79% at 615 nm) in Fig. 9(b) and for the PC71BM:GQD/P3HT nanofiber systems (53–55% at 606 nm) in Fig. 9(c). The Jsc values calculated from the EQE experiments, which were to some extent lower than those obtained from the J-V curves, are also reported in Table 1.

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data for all devices are fitted in a simple circuit with the charge transfer resistance (Rtr) on VersaSTAT-450, PAR. Nyquist plots were drawn as Z′ (real cell impedance) versus –Z″ (imaginary cell impedance). Fig. 11 (a-c) report the EIS data for the ternary and binary devices based on the P3HT and PC71BM as well as the GQD and CQD nanoparticles and their corresponding supramolecules at applied bias voltages (Vapp) of 0 mV and 900 mV under AM1.5G solar simulator at 100 mW cm2 light intensity. Fig. 12 also displays the horizontal bar graphs of Rtr variances for all ternary and binary systems. The impedance spectra for all devices were fitted by a simple circuit with the Rtr, chemical capacitance (Ctr), and series resistance (Rs) (Sharma et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2020). The intermediate frequency regime of the Nyquist plots was focused to analyze the recombination and charge transport. As reported in Fig. 11(a) and Table 1, the Rtr values of the ternary devices based on the CQD, CQD/P3HT short chain, CQD/P3HT long chain, CQD/P3HT nanofiber, GQD, GQD/P3HT short chain, GQD/P3HT long chain and GQD/P3HT nanofiber precursors were equal to 743, 668, 714, 553, 725, 462, 591 and 395 Ω.cm2, respectively. The values of Rtr for the fabricated solar cells are calculated using the inset model of Fig. 11. The systems modified by the crab/semicrab-like and decorated fibrillar nanostructures demonstrated the faster electron transport and thus the lower carrier recombination with respect to the other solar cells, as evidenced by the larger Jsc values tabulated in Table 1 (11.78, 12.89, 13.86 and 14.05 mA/cm2). In contrast, the binary photovoltaics led to very larger Rtr values, which was completely consistent with the previously recorded data. The worth data were recorded for the binary PC71BM:CQD (996 Ω.cm2) and PC71BM:GQD (994 Ω.cm2) devices. By using the pre-designed nanostructures as the electron donor and acceptor precursors in the binary systems, some partially acceptable results were also acquired including 664 Ω.cm2 for the P3HT:GQD/P3HT nanofiber and 781 Ω.cm2 for the P3HT:crab-like GQD/P3HT short chain photovoltaics (Fig. 11(b) and Table 1).

The crab-like GQD/P3HT and semicrab-like CQD/P3HT short chain, pellet-like GQD/P3HT and pellet-like CQD/P3HT long chain, GQD/P3HT nanofiber and CQD/P3HT nanofiber supramolecules were designed on the basis of the GQDs and CQDs. The P3HT crystals were composed of the face-on and edge-on oriented chains in all cases. The designed nanostructures were adopted in the ternary and binary photovoltaics of P3HT:PC71BM:supramolecule, P3HT:supramolecule, and PC71BM:supramolecule. The mature crab-like GQD/P3HT short chain supramolecules (5.47%) acted better than the immature semicrab-like CQD/P3HT short chain ones (3.46%) as the morphology modifiers. The pellet-like GQD/P3HT and CQD/P3HT long chain nanostructures (4.24% and 2.90%) were not also as effective as the crab-like and semicrab-like supramolecules. The better performance of GQD-based nanostructures compared with the CQD-based ones could be associated with the intrinsic nature of GQDs, possessing the thinner and disk-like configurations. The largest PCE of 6.50% was detected in P3HT:PC71BM:GQD/P3HT nanofiber solar cells (14.05 mA cm−2, 66.11%, 0.70 V, 8.6 × 10–3 cm2 V−1 s−1, 9.7 × 10–2 cm2 V−1 s−1). The highest photovoltaic characteristics among the CQD-based solar cells were also detected in the ternary P3HT:PC71BM:CQD/P3HT nanofiber systems, i.e., 12.89 mA cm−2, 58.01%, 0.61 V, 8.0 × 10–6 cm2 V−1 s−1, 9.3 × 10–5 cm2 V−1 s−1 and 4.56%. Furthermore, the low-quality binary photovoltaics were constructed on the basis of the designed nano-hybrids and P3HT or PC71BM precursors, i.e., 3.68% in P3HT:GQD/P3HT nanofiber, 2.29% in P3HT:crab-like GQD/P3HT short chain, 2.07% in P3HT:CQD/P3HT nanofiber, 2.01% in PC71BM:GQD/P3HT nanofiber, etc.
